speaker: interviewer
text: So now, how long did you stay in hiding in the little A-frame?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there till we-- we found out that the city was liberated by the Russian forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kosice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your own city.

speaker: interviewee
text: My own city, yeah. What happened is that after we were on our own, about seven or eight days, one night, after we were fast asleep, and we heard a terrible explosion. We thought maybe the German-- the fighting is close by, and that's the reason of that explosion. But it was just a single explosion, nothing else. We stayed to ourselves for a couple of days. And we didn't even go to get provisions. We were afraid to-- to go down to the city. Maybe-- maybe the fighting is close by. But then we ran out of food, and we-- I decided I'll go down to get some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After we went down, we were told that that hiding place, where they-- where we found refuge the first time, was raided by the Germans. They were-- some-- someone in the village must have-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --given it away to the Germans. And they came one night. They surrounded them. They took them-- they took them all, and they blew up that hiding place. And they took them down-- they took them to a-- to the city where I was-- my native city-- and they hanged them on-- on trees on the main street. So all six people were hanged. They were later exhumed and buried in-- in the Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was lucky that they kicked you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were lucky, and we felt so bad. You know, how could they have done to us, you know? It was well-organized there. They were eating well. They had good meals, and they were doing exercises. They were exercising and everything, [? yeah. ?] So that happened there. And towards-- towards the end of December, we had another incident. The-- the village drunkard, sort of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was-- who got friendly with one of the-- with a Russian partisan who parachuted into the forest and got lost from his unit-- befriended him. And, sort of, the drunkard must have heard from the other villagers where we bought our provisions and our blankets and everything else, that we had money. He must have told them that there is a way of getting some money from some rich people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came to our hiding place, that drunkard. He was armed with a hack. And the partisan had a handgun. First he was nice to us. We spoke Slovakian. Again, the Russian couldn't tell that-- you know, what we are. And the drunkard for sure couldn't tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted money from us. We told them, we have no money. We come from families of-- of-- modest [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people, modest-- we didn't have no money on us. And we were on-- on a state stipend, studying in Bratislava. For him, we told us we are students, you know. And we just don't have no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So since my brother looks much like myself, so he decided, the Russian, that he'll threaten one of us--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] --with harm, and maybe we'll give him the money. So he told my-- he told us-- he told my brother-- he'll take me, and if he'll have to, he'll kill me if I don't give the money. We didn't have-- you know, the only thing-- the only money we had was American currency. We had a $100 bill, and not too much Slovakian currency. And we needed the money to survive, so we stuck to our story. We don't have any money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took-- he grabbed me, and-- and he told my brother that-- my brother and I told him, I'm not-- we are not brothers, we are cousins. We just happen to resemble each other. But the resemblance is so striking, you know, that-- that he couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he took me down the road. We stopped at several-- before several trees, and he uncocked his handgun. And he told me, either you tell me where your money's hidden, or I'll kill you. Or I'll shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I just pleaded with him, that why don't you take me to your unit? You know, you have a-- I'll fight with you. I'm on your side. Why you-- why do you want to kill me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- if I would have money, I wouldn't offer myself. Even my brother would go and come and fight with you. Take-- take us to your unit. He says, well, he doesn't know where he unit is. He's on his own because he got lost from his unit. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He said maybe his unit perished. He doesn't know where they are. Anyway, he-- several times he stopped me, and he tried to-- tried to-- use me. I should tell him where the money is. At this point, I knew there is no end to it. Even if I show him-- if I give him-- you know, he would-- I don't have-- you know, I had maybe a couple of thousand [? kronen, ?] you know, and that $100 bill. It wouldn't help him. Finally, he-- finally, he-- we stopped again. And at that point I told him-- I-- he said he'll kill me if I don't tell him. He'll shoot me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him, I said, I don't-- why would you-- why would you blemish a victorious weapon with innocent blood? I said, I'm innocent. I said, I'm-- I never fought against you people. The other way around. We are on your side. We are Slavs just like you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, this isn't a Russian weapon, this is a captured German weapon. Then he decided he'll release me. He took-- I had a good coat that I brought from home. He took my coat in exchange for his ragged coat. It probably was warmer than my [LAUGHS] coat. And we-- he let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to my-- our hiding place. I found my brother praying the afternoon prayers and crying. [SOBBING] He didn't think that I'll ever come back, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then we stayed another-- at that-- point-- a couple of days later, we-- our friend, that pharmacist who had brought us up there, took us to the hiding place where his mother and her husband were hiding out. He didn't want to take us there because, supposedly, you know, even if-- had the Slovakians had found her, on a woman, you couldn't tell whether she is Jewish or not. And her husband was Czech. His name was Pohl, P-O-H-L. And the Slovaks hated the Czechs just as much as-- as-- almost as much as the Jews, but he wasn't Jewish. So they were hiding out. He didn't-- he never took us to his-- to that place. That place was-- was a much nicer place and a more comfortable place. They also had a radio, a battery-powered, big radio. And while we were there, we were-- he told us that the night-- last night, he was listening to Radio Moscow, the short-wave radio, and they announced that this native city was liberated. He was still afraid, since we were maybe 50 miles away from that city, or maybe less than 50 miles. We were afraid maybe they are still in retreat, and we-- we might be walking into their hands. So we stayed up another week. So did they. And then-- then January, the first week, just after Christmas-- I believe-- the reason I know it was after Christmas before-- because for Christmas, the villagers gave us a-- a chicken for Christmas. And we didn't pay for it. They gave it to us as a present, a Christmas present. And we didn't even know how to eat it, how to cook it, so. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came-- we came down. And we-- we were told that the Germans-- there are no Germans in-- in the next city, in Obysovce, and it's safe for us to come down. We stayed there for a couple of days, then they took us by wagon to the city of Obysovce. There, we-- we came by-- by wagon again. We hitched a ride. We wanted to hitch a ride back to our hometown. So we hitched some ride and we walked. Finally, I believe, in a couple of days-- it took us two days to get to our home city-- we came to the city. The streets were covered with snow. It was like a ghost town. You didn't see any person on the street. The men were taken by the Germans. I guess they took them to the trenches or whatever they're needed for. The women were left with children, and they were afraid to venture into the streets. They were afraid the-- from the-- of, of the German-- of the Russian soldiers. And even though it was a Slavic city, they were still afraid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But later on, the Czechoslovakian army [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occupied the barracks. And-- but still, the Russians-- if a Russian soldier saw a civilian, they tried to stop them, rob them of their watches or whatever they-- valuables they had. First, we went to our apartment. We found that the door was broken in. The furniture was there. The walls were bare. We had a lot of expensive paintings. One of the paintings still-- we still recovered. And my-- the twin paint was-- my brother has, and I have the other twin paint. It was painted by a valuable-- supposedly a valuable painting. After we saw our apartment, it was bare. We didn't find any clothing, and linen was all taken away. We went downstairs. We happened to find a fellow who was wearing my brother's good winter coat. We tried to ask [INAUDIBLE]. He denied. He said that's his own. And we were told to-- then we proceeded to the apartment of our-- of the nanny that brought us up, who was at that time married. We knocked on her door. She-- she was overjoyed to see us back. She didn't have-- she never had any children. She raised us. And she let us use-- we took a bath there. She washed our clothes. She put us up. She fed us. After-- after that, we stayed couple of days in her house. Still, we secured our apartment, and we were able to get some-- some bed linen and move into our apartment, it was-- although it was too big for us. But we-- the furniture-- all the furniture was there. We found some of our father's jewels hidden into-- inside the couch. They didn't think of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] looking there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually had-- had raided the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know. Probably the person who-- who lived-- there was one non-Jew, who was sort of like a superintendent of the apartment building, living there. And-- and he must have-- have raided the apartment, then. But that raid must have been done after the Germans left, and between just a very short period. We found the [? challah ?] that was probably opened up nine months ago, just before they took us-- my family away into the ghetto. We found some preserved tomato that my mother cooked and preserved in bottles. We found some pictures that were thrown all over the place. Nothing of value, just-- just things like that. And later on, we sort of managed to-- to put everything together. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you still had no money, so how were you eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- what we did is, we also had a camera, a Leica. That was a German, good camera that was, like, worth more than currency. At that point in time, we could use our $100-- we could get local currency. We could sell the $100 bill that we had to some other Jewish people. And we also-- so we bought-- I believe that Leica I bought in Prague. I used-- I traveled, and we used to barter, used to buy food and cigarettes and sell it to some black marketeers and-- and raise money. So we had money. And there was another incident that happened. I happened-- I brought a Leica from Prague, and that was probably worth $20. And in Hungary, in Kosice in Slovakia, it was worth more money. It was worth maybe $50. And that was a lot of money, the difference. So I tried to sell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So someone suggested that, you know, the Russian Orthodox priest-- Russian Orthodox priests are allowed to marry-- that he is in a market for a good camera. I should go and visit him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to his apartment. And all of a sudden I come into the house, and I see the paintings from our house-- about six or seven paintings-- that are all displayed on the walls. And so I told him, I said, you know, these are my parents' paintings. I want them back. He said, no problem. You can have it. And he bought my camera from me, too. He happened to be there with his girlfriend. He was divorced. He was getting a divorce from his first wife. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say how he got the paintings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't ask him. You know, I was glad that he was-- you know, he right away told me, you want to have them. So most of the paintings we recovered, and that's how I have one of the paintings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, this is January of 1944, but the war is still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is still going on. We tried to make ends meet by bartering, by-- by being on the black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry. 1945, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, right, yeah. And we are traveling-- I was traveling. My brother was younger. I had to leave Slovakia because I became-- in 1944 in September, I would have been 20 years old, and they would have taken me into the army. I had no intentions of joining the Czechoslovakian army. And so I was traveling in Hungary and Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not go back to your town, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back, but on false papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still-- are you still on the same papers that you had used all before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was on my-- I was-- I was using my youngest brother's papers, so actually, my papers said that I was 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. But with your real name.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- no, with my brother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, your brother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: --name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Family name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though I looked too old to be 14 or 15. But, you know, Slovakians, they were very simple people, and they were not as sophisticated as-- as Americans or-- or Germans were. And I was in-- in the city of [? Debrecen ?] when the war was over. In the meantime, my cousin, who lived in Brooklyn, who was a first cousin, who was born in the same city, found our names at the-- on the survivor's list. He-- she could write letters. She sent us currency, $10 bills, in the letter and also packages, whatever came through. And she was-- she wanted us-- she said, if no one else survived, she wants us to come to the States. There was no consular representation in the war zone in Slovakia, especially in-- in areas that were occupied by Russians. So we would-- in order to get to the United States, we would have had to go to a-- a country that had a continuous United States representation. Switzerland was out of the question. They wouldn't give us a visa unless they were sure that we could-- we could get out of-- and even Paris didn't have consular representation. They had delegation, and they had headquarters. So we had to go either to Spain or to a-- a country that had-- my cousins were very friendly with the Reichmanns of Toronto. You've heard of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, they lived in Tangier, Morocco. They were bankers there. So they asked them to arrange a visa to Tangier, Morocco. At that time, it was an international zone. We got-- they wired the visas to Prague. And in Prague, they arranged for us-- there was a relief organization, Vaad Hatzalah, in the United States, and that was my cousin-- that was founded by my cousin's brother-in-law. And they had in place in Prague offices where they were helping people to get out of Russia, Poland-- all these countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [INAUDIBLE] they arranged also for us to get-- it was out of question for me to get a Czechoslovakian passport. They wouldn't let us. They would have wanted us first to serve in their armed forces. So they arranged us to receive iden-- IDs from the Red Cross. And since we had a destination area where they would-- would take us in, like Tangier, we were able to get transit pass-- visas through Germany from the military authorities, and then transit visas in France and Spain, all the way to Tangier. And how long did you stay in Tangier?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tangier, we stayed for two-- two years. Had we arrived in-- after we arrived in Tangier, the legation wouldn't-- wouldn't give us any American visas unless we have a-- a legitimate passport. We contacted the consular-- Czechoslovakian consular authorities in London. They wouldn't-- they would only give us a passport-- I still have it-- to return to Czechoslovakia, but not to-- not to any other country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It took us till we-- it took us several months to find someone who had some connections with the Moroccan authorities-- French Moroccan authorities-- to give us a stateless passport. They issued us a stateless passport. By the time the passport was issued, the Czechoslovakian quarter was closed, so we couldn't come to the United States on that visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got stuck for two years. We-- we did some business in Morocco. We also-- we were very friendly with the-- we were friends with the Reichmann boys, who were our age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our friends, or our cousin, was trying to get somehow visas for us, but it took them two years to get an act of-- by-- by an act of-- a special act of Congress to allow us to come on a special visa. So it took us two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, briefly, bring us up to date how you-- you came to New York in-- on your visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- in May the 5th, 1948, we came to the-- New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you meet the woman who would become your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1952. We met through a mutual friend. It's her cousin and our-- and a friend of ours from our city who introduced us to each other. And we met in New York, and we got married November the 2nd, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: We would like very much to have an opportunity to meet your wife and some members of your family, and also to look at some of the photos that you were able to save from your family home. So we'll stop the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very nice family we've got here. OK, Victor, would you introduce some part of your family for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I would like you to meet my wife, Gloria, to my right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Bubby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Bubby, OK, Bubby, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I want you to-- the fellow sitting in my lap, his name is [? Eli. ?] The little girl sitting in Gloria's lap is Tzipi. Next to her, the baby, Yocheved. Then clockwise, Tzvi. That's Jeffrey's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why don't you point to them because we have to see them. These are all brothers and sisters, aren't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are twins, Tzvi and Esther. My daughter-in-law, Risa, my son, Jeff, my oldest son, Robert, holding his youngest son, Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: What a beautiful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and you have another son.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have another son who lives in New Jersey-- Teaneck, New Jersey. He has three lovely daughters. And my oldest son, Robert, has another boy by the name of David. And [? Chayaleh ?] and [? Dinah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren in all?

speaker: interviewee
text: All together, 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 grandchildren. Jeff, we're very glad that you could be with us on this tape. Robert appeared and talked to us about your mother when we had your mother's interview. Tell me what-- go ahead. You can go get your bear. Go pick up your bear. Pick up your bear.
